controls.BusinessRisk: Allowed sender domains are one of the most common misconfigurations in M365. If a permitted domain is compromised, all mail from it bypasses spam and malware filtering entirely.
controls.Remediation: Set-HostedContentFilterPolicy -Identity Default -AllowedSenderDomains @() — remove all allowed sender domains. Use mail flow rules for legitimate trusted partner exemptions with scoped conditions instead.

controls.Title: Meeting Recording Expiration Configured
controls.Description: Meeting recordings in Teams are configured to expire after 60-90 days rather than persisting indefinitely.
controls.BusinessRisk: Meeting recordings capture sensitive discussions, strategic planning, and personnel matters. Recordings that never expire accumulate indefinitely and expand the blast radius of any SharePoint/OneDrive compromise.
controls.Remediation: Set-CsTeamsMeetingPolicy -MeetingRecordingExpirationDays 60. Reference: MS.TEAMS.1.6v1 (SCuBA).

controls.Title: Anonymous Users Cannot Start Meetings
controls.Description: Anonymous (unauthenticated) users cannot independently start Teams meetings.
controls.BusinessRisk: If anonymous users can start meetings, attackers can open a meeting in an organization's tenant without any authenticated identity — creating an untracked communication channel.
controls.Remediation: Set-CsTeamsMeetingPolicy -AllowAnonymousUsersToStartMeeting $false. Reference: MS.TEAMS.2.2v1 (SCuBA).

controls.Title: Teams External Domain Federation Restricted to Allowlist
controls.Description: Teams federation with external domains is restricted to a defined allowlist rather than open to all domains.
controls.BusinessRisk: Open federation allows any Teams user at any organization worldwide to contact your users via Teams chat and calls. Social engineering and BEC campaigns can be delivered through Teams without going through email filtering.
controls.Remediation: Teams Admin Center > External access > Allow only specific external domains. Add business partner domains only. Reference: MS.TEAMS.1.3v1 (SCuBA).

controls.Title: Live Events Cannot Broadcast to Anonymous Internet Users
controls.Description: Teams Live Events are not configured to allow public anonymous streaming to unauthenticated internet users.
controls.BusinessRisk: Live events with anonymous broadcast enabled allow any internet user to view internal organizational meetings, presentations, and discussions without authentication.
controls.Remediation: Set-CsTeamsMeetingBroadcastPolicy -AllowBroadcastToAnonymousUsers $false. Reference: MS.TEAMS.1.7v2 (SCuBA).

controls.Title: Windows LAPS Deployed via Intune
controls.Description: Windows Local Administrator Password Solution (LAPS) is deployed via Intune, generating unique randomized local admin passwords escrowed in Entra ID. (No SCuBA equivalent — Intune device-compliance controls are out of SCuBA scope.)
controls.BusinessRisk: Shared or static local admin passwords enable pass-the-hash lateral movement — one compromised endpoint hands the attacker credentials that work on every other endpoint with the same password.
controls.Remediation: Intune > Endpoint security > Account protection > Create Windows LAPS policy. Configure: Admin account managed = Yes, Password age = 30 days, Password complexity = Large letters + small letters + numbers + special characters.
controls.LicenseRequirement: M365 Business Premium or Intune Plan 1

controls.Title: Windows Hello for Business Deployed
controls.Description: Windows Hello for Business is deployed via Intune policy, enabling phishing-resistant passwordless authentication on enrolled Windows endpoints. (No SCuBA equivalent — NLS/CIS hardening beyond SCuBA scope.)
controls.BusinessRisk: WHfB eliminates password-based credential theft on enrolled endpoints — LSASS dumping yields nothing, phishing yields nothing. It is the highest-value passwordless deployment available at no extra license cost.
controls.Remediation: Intune > Devices > Windows > Windows enrollment > Windows Hello for Business settings. Or deploy via endpoint protection policy: Use Windows Hello for Business = Enabled.
controls.LicenseRequirement: M365 Business Premium or Intune Plan 1

controls.Title: Device OS Version Compliance Enforced
controls.Description: Device compliance policies enforce minimum OS versions and compliance rate is ≥90% across enrolled devices. (No SCuBA equivalent — Intune device-compliance controls are out of SCuBA scope.)
controls.BusinessRisk: Endpoints running outdated OS versions contain unpatched known exploits. KEV-listed vulnerabilities are actively exploited by ransomware operators and nation-state actors.
controls.Remediation: Intune > Devices > Compliance policies > Create policy > OS minimum version = latest-1. Review noncompliant devices report and address outliers.
controls.LicenseRequirement: M365 Business Premium or Intune Plan 1

controls.Title: Mobile Device Compliance Requires PIN or Biometric
controls.Description: iOS and Android compliance policies require a PIN or biometric lock before accessing managed apps. (No SCuBA equivalent — NLS/CIS hardening beyond SCuBA scope.)
controls.BusinessRisk: Unlocked mobile devices give physical access to all managed apps, corporate email, Teams conversations, and OneDrive files — no attacker credential required.
controls.Remediation: Intune > Devices > Compliance > iOS/Android policy > System security > Require password = Require. Set minimum length = 6, complexity = Numeric + symbols.
controls.LicenseRequirement: M365 Business Premium or Intune Plan 1

controls.Title: Microsoft Purview Audit (Premium) Enabled
controls.Description: Advanced Audit (now Microsoft Purview Audit Premium) is enabled for all users, capturing high-value events including MailItemsAccessed.
controls.BusinessRisk: Standard audit does not capture MailItemsAccessed — the event that tells you which emails a compromised account read during a breach. Without it, mail exfil investigations are blind.
controls.Remediation: Assign Microsoft 365 E5 or E5 Compliance license to users, then enable Audit Premium: Purview > Audit > Audit log retention policies. Verify MailItemsAccessed events appear in audit search.
controls.LicenseRequirement: M365 E5 or E5 Compliance add-on

controls.Title: Audit Log Retention Policy Extends to 1 Year or More
controls.Description: A custom audit log retention policy preserves audit logs for at least 365 days for the highest-risk user populations.
controls.BusinessRisk: Breaches have a median detection time over 200 days. The default 90-day retention window means the forensic record for most breaches has been purged before the breach is even detected.
controls.Remediation: Purview > Audit > Audit log retention policies > New retention policy. Priority: All activities, Users: All, Duration: 1 year or longer. Requires Audit Premium.
controls.LicenseRequirement: M365 E5 or E5 Compliance add-on

controls.Title: Sensitivity Labels Defined and Published
controls.Description: Sensitivity labels are configured in Purview and published via label policies to users across all workloads.
controls.BusinessRisk: Without sensitivity labels, data has no classification context. DLP policies that depend on label conditions cannot fire, and information protection policies have nothing to act on.
controls.Remediation: Purview > Information protection > Sensitivity labels > Create label hierarchy (Public, Internal, Confidential, Highly Confidential). Create label policy and publish to all users.

controls.Title: Records Management Labels Configured for Regulated Data
controls.Description: Records management retention labels are configured for content subject to legal or regulatory hold requirements. (No SCuBA equivalent — NLS/CIS hardening beyond SCuBA scope.)
controls.BusinessRisk: Without immutable records declarations, content subject to litigation hold can be modified or deleted — creating legal liability and spoliation risk.
controls.Remediation: Purview > Records management > Labels > Create record label with retention action. Publish via label policy. Required for healthcare (HIPAA), government, and financial organizations.

controls.Title: M365 Copilot Sensitivity Label Enforcement
controls.Description: Sensitivity labels are published with Files & emails scope and an auto-labeling policy is active, enabling Microsoft 365 Copilot to respect data classification boundaries when grounding on tenant content. (No SCuBA equivalent — NLS/CIS hardening beyond SCuBA scope.)
controls.BusinessRisk: Without sensitivity labels, Copilot can access and surface any data the user can reach — including HR files, financial records, and confidential strategies — with no awareness of sensitivity. Copilot dramatically amplifies over-sharing risk.
controls.Remediation: Purview > Information protection > Labels > Create/verify labels with Files & emails scope. Enable Copilot integration in label settings. Reference: Microsoft Copilot security baseline.
controls.LicenseRequirement: M365 Business Premium or E3+ (Copilot requires M365 Copilot add-on)

controls.Title: DLP Policy Active for Copilot Interactions
controls.Description: At least one enabled Data Loss Prevention policy includes the Microsoft 365 Copilot location, blocking sensitive-information types in Copilot prompts and responses. (No SCuBA equivalent — NLS/CIS hardening beyond SCuBA scope.)
controls.BusinessRisk: Without DLP covering Copilot, users can extract sensitive data by prompting Copilot to summarize, translate, or reformat confidential content — bypassing all other data controls.
controls.Remediation: Purview > DLP > Create policy > Locations: Include Microsoft 365 Copilot. Add sensitive info types as conditions. Requires M365 E5 Compliance or equivalent.

controls.Title: Copilot Access Restricted to Licensed Users
controls.Description: Microsoft 365 Copilot license assignment is scoped to a defined subset of users with documented business need — not blanket-assigned tenant-wide. (No SCuBA equivalent — NLS/CIS hardening beyond SCuBA scope.)
controls.BusinessRisk: Uncontrolled Copilot access means unlicensed users may gain Copilot capabilities through shared accounts or feature rollouts, bypassing governance controls.
controls.Remediation: M365 Admin Center > Settings > Microsoft 365 Copilot > Control access. Assign licenses only to approved users with a documented business need.

controls.Title: Copilot Studio Agent Publishing Governed
controls.Description: Copilot Studio (Power Virtual Agents) bots are not externally published to anonymous channels; bot publisher domains are confined to the tenant's onmicrosoft.com domain. (No SCuBA equivalent — NLS/CIS hardening beyond SCuBA scope.)
controls.BusinessRisk: Ungoverned Copilot Studio agents can be built to query sensitive SharePoint libraries, connect to external APIs, and respond to any user in the organization — creating unreviewed data exposure channels at scale.
controls.Remediation: Power Platform Admin Center > Copilot Studio > Policies > Require admin approval for agent publishing. Apply DLP policies to Copilot Studio environment connectors.

controls.Title: Copilot Interaction Audit Logging Active
controls.Description: Unified Audit Log ingestion is enabled and a Purview retention policy explicitly covers Copilot interactions so prompts/responses are searchable beyond platform-default retention. (No SCuBA equivalent — NLS/CIS hardening beyond SCuBA scope.)
controls.BusinessRisk: Without Copilot interaction logging, an insider using Copilot to extract sensitive data leaves no audit trail — the interaction is functionally invisible to security and compliance teams.
controls.Remediation: Ensure Unified Audit Log is enabled (Set-AdminAuditLogConfig -UnifiedAuditLogIngestionEnabled $true). Copilot interactions are searchable in Purview > Content explorer and Audit > Search.

controls.Title: Users Without MFA — Named List
controls.Description: Identifies every enabled user account with no MFA method registered by name and UPN.
controls.BusinessRisk: Each account without MFA can be compromised with a stolen password alone. A single phished credential gives an attacker full mailbox access, Teams, SharePoint, and the ability to initiate wire transfers or BEC campaigns.
controls.Remediation: Entra ID > Users > [select user] > Authentication methods > Add method. For bulk: Get-MgUser | where MFA not registered → require combined registration via CA policy targeted at these users.

controls.Title: Stale Guest Accounts — Named List
controls.Description: Identifies guest accounts with no sign-in activity in 90+ days.
controls.BusinessRisk: Stale guest accounts from former vendors, contractors, and partners retain access to SharePoint sites, Teams channels, and any resource they were granted. Ex-partners can exfiltrate data months after their engagement ends.
controls.Remediation: Entra ID > External Identities > All users > Filter by guest, sort by last sign-in. Disable or delete accounts inactive 90+ days. Implement access reviews: PIM > Access reviews > Create review for guest users.
controls.LicenseRequirement: Included (all plans) — Access Reviews require Entra P2

controls.Title: Stale Licensed Member Accounts — Named List
controls.Description: Identifies licensed member accounts with no sign-in activity in 90+ days — likely departed employees. (No SCuBA equivalent — NLS/CIS hardening beyond SCuBA scope.)
controls.BusinessRisk: Active accounts for departed employees are a primary threat vector. Former employees know the organization's structure, workflows, and email conventions — making them highly effective social engineers if accounts are ever used maliciously.
controls.Remediation: Verify each account represents an active user. Disable accounts for departed employees immediately. Revoke all active sessions. Remove assigned licenses. Review for any recent inbox rules or forwarding.

controls.Title: OAuth Apps with Tenant-Wide Consent — Named List
controls.Description: Identifies all applications that have been granted OAuth permissions across ALL users (AllPrincipals consent type).
controls.BusinessRisk: Apps with AllPrincipals consent can access every user's mailbox, files, calendar, and identity. Consent phishing campaigns trick admins into granting these permissions to malicious apps. Many legitimate apps accumulate excessive scopes over time.
controls.Remediation: Entra ID > Enterprise applications > All applications > Filter by permissions. Review each AllPrincipals grant. Revoke unnecessary grants: Remove-MgOAuth2PermissionGrant -OAuth2PermissionGrantId <id>.

controls.Title: Mailboxes with External Email Forwarding — Named List
controls.Description: Identifies every mailbox with a ForwardingSmtpAddress set to an external domain.
controls.BusinessRisk: External email forwarding is the #1 BEC persistence technique. An attacker who compromises a mailbox sets forwarding to maintain access even after a password reset. Forwarding to a personal account also creates an unauthorized data copy outside organizational control.
controls.Remediation: For each listed mailbox: verify the forwarding is intentional and business-justified. Remove unauthorized: Set-Mailbox -Identity <upn> -ForwardingSmtpAddress $null. Also set transport rule to block auto-forward and create an alert policy for new forwarding rules.

controls.Title: Shared Mailboxes with Sign-In Enabled — Named List
controls.Description: Identifies shared mailboxes where the associated user account has interactive sign-in enabled.
controls.BusinessRisk: Shared mailboxes are designed to be accessed via delegation, not direct login. An enabled shared mailbox account bypasses the per-user MFA requirement — it's a permanent backdoor into email with no authentication strength requirement.
controls.Remediation: Set-AzureADUser -ObjectId <guid> -AccountEnabled $false for each shared mailbox account. Or in M365 Admin: Active users > select user > Block sign-in. Verify delegates can still access via Outlook.

controls.Title: Mailboxes with Audit Logging Disabled — Named List
controls.Description: Identifies mailboxes with AuditEnabled explicitly set to $false.
controls.BusinessRisk: A compromised mailbox with audit disabled cannot be investigated. After a BEC incident, forensics cannot determine what emails were read, what rules were created, who accessed the mailbox, or when the compromise began.
controls.Remediation: Get-Mailbox -ResultSize Unlimited | Where-Object { $_.AuditEnabled -eq $false } | Set-Mailbox -AuditEnabled $true

controls.Title: Per-User SMTP AUTH Override — Named List
controls.Description: Identifies users with individual SMTP AUTH enabled, bypassing the org-level SMTP AUTH disable.
controls.BusinessRisk: SMTP AUTH does not support MFA. Users with per-user SMTP AUTH enabled can authenticate to Exchange via legacy protocols with just a password — bypassing all Conditional Access policies and MFA requirements.
controls.Remediation: For each listed user: Set-CASMailbox -Identity <upn> -SmtpClientAuthenticationDisabled $true. If SMTP AUTH is required (printer, scanner, legacy app) use a dedicated service account with a strong password and IP restriction.

controls.Title: Microsoft Secure Score
controls.Description: Microsoft's own assessment of the tenant's security posture across identity, data, apps, and devices. (No SCuBA equivalent — NLS/CIS hardening beyond SCuBA scope.)
controls.BusinessRisk: Microsoft Secure Score measures the same controls through Microsoft's lens. A score below 50% indicates fundamental security gaps across the tenant. It is independently verifiable and useful for trend tracking.
controls.Remediation: Review Microsoft Secure Score improvement actions at security.microsoft.com > Secure Score. Prioritize Identity and Email improvement actions first.

controls.Title: No Mailbox Forwarding to External Addresses
controls.Description: No mailboxes have a ForwardingSmtpAddress configured to an external recipient. Server-side mailbox forwarding is a common BEC persistence channel that silently exfiltrates all inbound mail.
controls.BusinessRisk: ForwardingSmtpAddress is set at the mailbox level and survives password resets and license changes. Attackers who compromise a single mailbox use it to maintain visibility into the victim's mail after the initial intrusion is contained. It is also a common insider exfil vector for departing employees.
controls.Remediation: Get-Mailbox -ResultSize Unlimited -Filter "ForwardingSmtpAddress -ne `$null" | Set-Mailbox -ForwardingSmtpAddress $null -ForwardingAddress $null -DeliverToMailboxAndForward $false. Also block at transport layer: Set-RemoteDomain Default -AutoForwardEnabled $false.

controls.Title: No Inbox Rules Forwarding Externally
controls.Description: No mailbox inbox rules use ForwardTo, RedirectTo, or ForwardAsAttachmentTo to deliver mail to external recipients. Inbox rules are the classic post-credential-compromise persistence channel (MITRE T1114.003).
controls.BusinessRisk: Inbox rules created by an attacker after credential compromise will quietly forward mail of interest (finance, executives, password resets) to an attacker-controlled address. They bypass ForwardingSmtpAddress monitoring and survive sign-in revocation if the rule was created before the revoke.
controls.Remediation: Audit: foreach ($m in Get-Mailbox -ResultSize Unlimited) { Get-InboxRule -Mailbox $m.UserPrincipalName | Where-Object { $_.ForwardTo -or $_.RedirectTo -or $_.ForwardAsAttachmentTo } }. Disable: Disable-InboxRule -Mailbox <UPN> -Identity <RuleName>. Block at transport: Set-RemoteDomain Default -AutoForwardEnabled $false.

controls.Title: No Mailboxes with Per-User Audit Explicitly Disabled
controls.Description: No mailboxes have AuditEnabled = $false set explicitly. As of January 2019, mailbox audit is on by default org-wide; explicit per-user disable is a deliberate override and almost always wrong.
controls.BusinessRisk: Mailboxes with audit explicitly disabled cannot be investigated after a compromise. Inbox-rule creation, mail access by delegates, and sent-item modification are not captured for the disabled mailbox — a deliberate or accidental blind spot that thwarts incident response.
controls.Remediation: Get-Mailbox -ResultSize Unlimited | Where-Object { $_.AuditEnabled -eq $false } | Set-Mailbox -AuditEnabled $true. For a single mailbox: Set-Mailbox -Identity <UPN> -AuditEnabled $true.

controls.Title: No Per-User SMTP AUTH Overrides
controls.Description: No mailboxes have SmtpClientAuthenticationDisabled = $false set at the mailbox level. Per-user overrides bypass the tenant-level SMTP AUTH disable and re-enable basic authentication for that mailbox.
controls.BusinessRisk: Per-user SMTP AUTH exceptions re-enable basic auth for the affected mailboxes — bypassing MFA and Conditional Access. They are a classic password-spray and credential-stuffing target, and they are usually granted ad-hoc for legacy scanners or line-of-business apps and then forgotten.
controls.Remediation: For each affected mailbox: Set-CASMailbox -Identity <UPN> -SmtpClientAuthenticationDisabled $true. Migrate senders to OAuth-based SMTP (Microsoft Graph sendMail API) or App Passwords with MFA. For multifunction devices/scanners, prefer SMTP relay via on-prem connector with IP allowlist, or Direct Send (anonymous) — neither requires basic auth.
